text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

Scarlett Mew Jensen and Yasmin Harper picked up bronze before Anna Henderson's surprise silver in the women's time trial

PARIS — Anna Henderson earnedTeam GB’sfirst silver of the Games with a narrow second place in the women’s individual time-trial cycling.

Henderson finished with a time of 41:09.83 behind Australia’s Grace Brown, eclipsing American Chloe Dygert by just a second. It has been a long road to glory for Brown, who has twice finished as runner-up at World Championships but this time was in a league of her own, eclipsing Henderson by a minute and a half.

The British rider was in fact something of a latecomer to cycling, and excelled as a national champion in skiing as a youngster, which perhaps helped her stay upright in treacherous conditions.

“I didn’t realise how slippery it was out there until I was already on the course,” Henderson said afterwards.

“I thought ‘I could lose the whole Olympic Games in one corner here’ and you gain all your time in the straights. So I really took control and my coach did a really good job to keep me calm the whole way through.

“Last year I was two seconds from the bronze in worlds so I’m really happy to be on the right side of the seconds this time.”

Her silver added to what had already been a special day for British athletes, with Scarlett Mew Jensen and Yasmin Harper picking up the nation’s first medal – a bronze – in the 3m springboard synchronised diving event.

They claimed it thanks to a “shocking” blow-up from Australia to earn GB’s first opening-day medal in 20 years, since Pete Waterfield and Leon Taylor’s silver in the 10m sychro at Athens.

Harper – on her Olympic debut – and Mew Jensen were in fourth place going into the final dive of the morning and their score of 70.68 for their three-and-a-half pike somersault did not look enough.

But then came Maddison Keeney and Annabele Smith and their two-and-a-half somersault twist, a small degree of difficulty lower than the British pair.

And they landed an audible distance from synchronisation: the two cracks as they hit the water was easily noticeable. The crowd were stunned into silence – one Australian journalist exclaimed an elongated “f**k”.

Mew Jensen and Harper still had three more dives to watch before the medal was confirmed – and when it was, the pair leapt and hugged their coaching staff. They now have an Olympic medal to match their bronze at last year’s World Championships.

Mew Jensen said: “We knew that even after our last dive that we would still be on the back foot and we knew that Australia needed to mess up. For that to actually happen, we were very, very shocked because that is a very easy dive for them, they are very talented, very experienced.

“It was very, very shocking. But we needed to deliver towards the end.”

Harper, who turns 24 on Monday, added: “We knew it was going to be tight and to watch them not perform on the last round… I think I knew straight away [after the Australians’ dive] that it wasn’t enough. But you are still waiting for that scoreboard to pop up.”

It was GB’s first ever medal at this discipline – and their first female diving medal in 64 years. It underlines the depth of talent in the sport.

We still have the likes of Andrea Spendolini-Sirieux, Tom Daley and Jack Laugher to come at the aquatic centre. The Australians, meanwhile, will want to forget this day.

China were untouchable and close to flawless, with Yani Chang and Yiwen Chen finishing 23 points ahead of USA’s Sarah Bacon and Kassidy Cook.

title: Team GB bag first medals of Paris Olympics in diving and cycling
author: Matt Butler
description: Scarlett Mew Jensen and Yasmin Harper picked up bronze before Anna Henderson's surprise silver in the women's time trial
